coredata.dc:description: In recent years, zinc oxide (ZnO)-based methyl ammonium lead halide perovskite photovoltaic (PPv) technologies have achieved high power conversion efficiency. However, several key factors have hindered the commercialization of PPv devices. We report a ZnO electron transport layer surface decorated with solution-processable carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which significantly enhances interfacial charge transport between the perovskite and electron transport layer (ETL) interface. With a simple synthesis method, the ETL surface was coated with CQDs. Waste coffee grounds mixed with water, ethanol, and n-hexane solvents were used to extract carbon quantum dots of various sizes, whose optical, structural, and morphological properties were confirmed by a variety of sophisticated characterizations. The PPv device exhibited an increase in power conversion efficiency (PCE) from 9.09 % to 11.71 %, due to charge extraction. Especially, the planar perovskite photovoltaic (PPv) device with an 8 nm-sized CQDs-ZnO hybrid layer attained the degradation percentages of 16.39 %, 8.66 %, 5.87 %, and 1.96 %, for ZnO, E-CQDs, nH-CQDs, and W-CQDs, respectively. This work provides a cost-effective and eco-friendly with ZnO are a promising candidate for planar PPv structures.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly carbon quantum dots from coffee waste: Improving charge transfer in solid-state perovskite solar cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, zinc oxide (ZnO)-based methyl ammonium lead halide perovskite photovoltaic (PPv) technologies have achieved high power conversion efficiency. However, several key factors have hindered the commercialization of PPv devices. We report a ZnO electron transport layer surface decorated with solution-processable carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which significantly enhances interfacial charge transport between the perovskite and electron transport layer (ETL) interface. With a simple synthesis method, the ETL surface was coated with CQDs. Waste coffee grounds mixed with water, ethanol, and n-hexane solvents were used to extract carbon quantum dots of various sizes, whose optical, structural, and morphological properties were confirmed by a variety of sophisticated characterizations. The PPv device exhibited an increase in power conversion efficiency (PCE) from 9.09 % to 11.71 %, due to charge extraction. Especially, the planar perovskite photovoltaic (PPv) device with an 8 nm-sized CQDs-ZnO hybrid layer attained the degradation percentages of 16.39 %, 8.66 %, 5.87 %, and 1.96 %, for ZnO, E-CQDs, nH-CQDs, and W-CQDs, respectively. This work provides a cost-effective and eco-friendly with ZnO are a promising candidate for planar PPv structures.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The current energy landscape is hazardous owing to growing global demands, the exhaustion of non-renewable resources, and increasing pollution challenges. Since light energy can be directly converted into electricity, it is one of the most potent renewable sources of energy [1,2]. Third-generation solar cells offer benefits such as simple manufacturing processes, high efficiency under various lighting conditions, low toxicity, and cost-effectiveness [3]. Developing perovskite solar cells (PSCs) has demonstrated diverse photovoltaic materials. However, bulk-sized nanomaterials can reduce the electrical and charge transport properties in negative intrinsic positive (n-i-p) and inverted positive intrinsic negative (p-i-n) structured sandwiched perovskite solar cells [4]. Various modifications have been employed to enhance the longevity of PSCs, reduce recombination losses, and improve charge extraction at the perovskite/electron transport layer (ETL) interface. Quantum dots (QDs) have enriched PSC performance [5].

In PSC architecture, electron transport materials (ETMs), perovskites, and hole transport materials play a significant role, with ETMs being pivotal. ETMs facilitate the transport of photogenerated electrons, prevent hole recombination, and regulate interface energy levels, forming the basis for good-quality perovskite solar cells. Commonly used materials in PSCs include TiO2, ZnO, SnO2, and Nb2O5 [6–8]. Recently, ZnO has emerged as a promising ETM owing to its suitable ~3.4 eV band gap, greater charge carrier mobility (2.05 × 102 cm2/V/s), and hexagonal crystal structure, making it ideal for photovoltaics [9]. Commonly, the light-harvesting ability of ZnO, and their rapid impact on perovskite grain boundaries, reduce PSCs' performance and stability.

To address these issues, researchers have focused on ZnO-based interfacial surface modifications in PSCs. This is because the ZnO surface, under light conditions, produces reactive oxygen species that adversely affect the perovskite crystal lattice. These easily reduced the device's performance and lifetime. These modification strategies, prevent moisture and boost the charge extractions enhancing the PSCs' performance and stability. Commonly, interfacial surface modifiers act as various materials, such as ionic liquids (1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate (BMIMPF6), 1-Methyl-3-propylimidazolium phosphate (MPIMPH)), inorganic (dual layer, nanocomposite, and plasmonic particles), organic, and carbon materials (graphene oxide, C60) are widely employed in PSCs [10–14]. These modified material preparation and device construction processes have more expensive and high manufacturing costs and waste by-products chemicals affect the environment and living things [15–17].

Alternatively, QDs have been used in perovskite photovoltaic applications owing to their distinct photophysical and electrical features. The composition, size, shape, and surface chemistry of quantum dots majorly control their properties. Their outstanding characteristics, such as size-dependent tunable band gaps, exceptional photostability, quantum confinement effects, high absorption coefficients, water solubility, low environmental impact, and the ability to produce multiple excitons, make them a focal point in research for energy conversion applications. Predominantly, II-VI and III-V quantum dots have been utilized in ETL modifications in recent years. However, these hazardous heavy metal QDs pose environmental and health risks. Thus, there has been a shift towards eco-friendly QDs, in line with sustainable goals. To address this, green synthesis-based CQDs have been developed as reliable, persistent, and environmentally safe alternatives [18,19]. Among them, sp
2 hybrid CQDs show excellent function in improving band alignment and charge extraction of the ETL/perovskite interface. Moreover, CQDs surface integrated carboxyl (-COOH), oxygen (O2), and hydroxyl (-OH) functional groups from different preparation methods, which helped to minimize surface defects and plays an active center for non-radiative recombination to avoid charge transfer and extraction on the perovskite layer and influence its crystallization process.

Commonly, CQDs act as interfacial modifiers or additives in the ETL or within the perovskite layer itself. CQDs can enhance both the efficiency and stability of organometallic perovskite solar cells. By filling grain boundaries after decorating the ETM surface, CQDs reduce short-circuit damage and prevent the infiltration of perovskite material into the ETL grain boundary interfaces. Additionally, CQDs must minimize or avoid charge recombination at the ETL/perovskite interface. An ETL should have a valence band maximum position higher than that of the perovskite to enhance the open circuit voltage (Voc) of the PSCs [20]. Suppressing non-radiative recombination, improving band alignment, and enhancing charge extraction are key advancements in perovskite solar cell construction. These developments include the use of CQD-modified ETLs, perovskite layers, and ETL/perovskite interfaces [15]. Previously, various properties of CQDs-modified ETL were reported, such as CQDs/TiOx [21], amino-rich CQDs/SnO2, [22] and N and Cl co-doped CQDs achieving high efficiency and stability. CQDs modified ETL thickness significantly influences series resistance, affecting the fill factor [23,24]. Particularly, low-temperature, solvents-based CQDs modified PPv have been developed in recent years due to low energy consumption. Above absorbed from the literature, the CQDs properties, and device performance are discussed. The effects of CQD size, solvent variations, and device performance are discussed very little in previous kinds of literature. Some of the reported works of literature based on CQD with semiconducting oxide materials as follows, it is noted that the solution-processable ZnO/carbon quantum dots achieved the efficiency of 9.64 % which is >27 % of initial efficiency (9.64 %) reported by Ruqin Zhang [25]. Mohammad Mahdi Tavakoli et al. stated that ZnO/rGO QDs-based PSCs achieved the maximum PCE of 15.2 % [26]. It is noted that Jing Liu et al. reported that CQDs embedded in SnO2 in PSCs achieved an efficiency of 24.05 % and retained over 84 % of their initial PCE [27]. Longkai Yang et al. reported that exfoliated fluorographene QDs achieved a PCE of 20.40 % [28]. While ZnO is a promising ETL due to its high electron mobility (2.05 × 102 cm2/V/s) and suitable bandgap (~3.4 eV), its inherent surface defects and photocatalytic instability under UV exposure lead to perovskite degradation and reduced device stability. To overcome these issues, we introduce CQDs as an interfacial modifier, which passivates defects, enhances charge extraction, and mitigates ZnO-induced degradation, thereby improving both efficiency and stability in PSCs. This revision will ensure a smoother transition between ZnO's strengths and limitations.

In this study, different-sized CQDs are synthesized from waste coffee grounds using a sustainable approach with water, ethanol, and n-hexane as solvents. The extracted CQDs are introduced as modifiers to spin-coated ZnO ETL, enhancing its optical and electrical properties while tuning the interfacial properties between the ETL and CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite. It is demonstrated that CQDs-ZnO reduces surface defects and improves Voc. Planar PSCs with an 8 nm CQDs-ZnO hybrid ETL achieved a PCE of 11.27 %, surpassing the 9.09 % PCE of devices using a single ZnO ETL, with much-improved operational stability. Further investigation revealed that CQDs enhance electronic conductivity for efficient charge transfer and can passivate defects in ZnO. Notably, PSCs with CQDs-ZnO maintained over 10.32 % efficiency after 500 h of continuous testing under AM 1.5 G lamp irradiation. This work offers a simple and feasible approach to boost the ETL performance, which could benefit not only planar PSCs but also other photoelectric devices.

The waste coffee powder (WCP) was collected from a nearby coffee shop (12.9977° N, 80.0972° E, Kundrathur, Chennai). Materials such as n-hexane (95 %), ethanol (>99 %), lead iodide (PbI₂, 99 %), methylammonium iodide (CH₃NH₃I, 98 %), dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8 %), fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrates (7 Ω/sq), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.9 %), and 0.22 μm polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) filters were purchased from Merck, India. Low-temperature carbon paste was purchased from Solaronix, Switzerland. To dialyze quantum dots, a Himedia dialysis membrane-70 with a cut-off of 500–1000 Da was used. The analysis employed deionized water from the Millipore Direct-Q Ultrapure water system (Milli-Q). All chemicals were of analytical grade and purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Private Limited, Bangalore, India. Fig. 1 shows that synthesizing procedure of CQDs from commercial coffee powder.

The FTO glass substrate was initially cleaned with detergent and water, followed by ultrasonic cleaning in acetone, isopropanol, and ethanol for 30 min. After cleaning, the glass was transferred to a heating plate for drying. The FTO glass was then subjected to UV treatment for 15 min. Subsequently, the FTO/ZnO/CQDs/CH₃NH₃PbI₃/Carbon structure was fabricated under room temperature (RT) conditions. Device fabrication parameters such as spin speed and annealing duration were optimized through extensive experimentation. To ensure reproducibility and statistical accuracy, performance metrics were averaged over five independently fabricated devices.

The ZnO paste was prepared using commercial ZnO nanoparticles (1.12 g), which were mixed with terpineol and ethyl cellulose in a 1:2 ratio. The mixture was ground continuously using a mortar and pestle until a white paste was formed at room temperature (RT) for 2 h. A few drops of ethanol were then added to the ZnO paste to make it suitable for the spin-coating process. The ZnO solution was spin-coated onto the cleaned FTO glass at 8000 rpm for 60 s, and the resulting FTO/ZnO films were annealed at 400 °C for 25 min. After cooling to RT, a W-CQDs aqueous solution of a specific concentration was spin-coated onto the FTO/ZnO at 7000 rpm for 30 s, followed by thermal annealing at 100 °C for 10 min under ambient conditions. This process successfully yielded the FTO/ZnO/W-CQDs hybrid electron transport layer structure. The same procedure was followed to fabricate the FTO/ZnO/E-CQDs and FTO/ZnO/H-CQDs hybrid electron transport layers.

To prepare the CH₃NH₃PbI₃ perovskite solution, a previously reported procedure [28] was followed. Specifically, 1.23 M of PbI2 and 1.54 M of CH3NH3I were dissolved in 3 mL of a DMF/DMSO solvent mixture with a volume ratio of 9:1. The solution was stirred at 900 rpm for 24 h to obtain a clear perovskite solution, which was then spin-coated (3000 rpm for 30 s) onto the ZnO/CQDs electron transport layer at RT. The deposited perovskite film was subsequently annealed at 110 °C for 25 min.

Commercial low-temperature carbon paste was used for the electrode fabrication process. For the perovskite layer deposition, the carbon electrode was blade-coated onto the CH3NH3PbI3 film at 120 °C for 30 min. This resulted in an n-i-p structured device with the configuration FTO/ZnO/CQDs/CH₃NH₃PbI₃/Carbon. The active area of the fabricated perovskite solar cell device was 0.5 cm2.

UV–visible absorbance studies were carried out using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Model: Hach USA DR6000). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using the Bruker D8 Advance model. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted using an FT-IR spectrometer (IR Affinity-1, Shimadzu). The 3D fluorescence scan was analyzed using a 3D excitation-emission matrix (EEM, Hitachi Fluorescence Spectrophotometer-F7000) with the following settings: 150 W xenon arc lamp, 450 V applied voltage, 12,000 nm/min scanning speed, 5 nm scanning interval, 5 nm slit width, 1 cm quartz cuvette path length, and an excitation/emission wavelength range of 200–600 nm. This instrument was also used to measure the photoluminescence (PL) of the S-CQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 3010) was employed to determine the morphology of the CQDs. Particle size analysis from the TEM images was processed using IMAGEJ software. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using the ULVAC-PHI apparatus (PHI5000 Version). Raman analysis was carried out using a HORIBA LabRAM instrument. The J–V characteristics of the fabricated 0.5 cm2 perovskite device were analyzed using an Oriel solar simulator with AM 1.5 sun irradiation.

XRD is an excellent non-destructive structural technique used to examine the waste coffee powder extract-derived CQDs' crystalline structure. The XRD pattern of W-CQDs, E-CQDs, and nH-CQDs is shown in Fig. 2 . It is observed that the there is a single broad diffraction peaks of plane (002) at 23.560, 22.670, 22.570 and 22.680 corresponds to WCP, W-CQDs, E-CQDs and nH-CQDs, respectively. The diffraction peak is well matches with standard JCPDS (41–1487) of graphite. This indicates a lattice spacing distance (dhkl) of 3.93 Å. Moreover, the W-CQDs exhibited higher broadness and slight changes in intensity compared to the other CQD samples, primarily due to variations in crystallite size, which significantly influenced the stress and strain effects. These indicated the turbostratic structure resulting from the interlayer clustering of disordered atoms of carbon in CQDs' hexagonal graphite structures. Similar type of results were reported by S. Marouzi et al. [29], Ayushi kundu et al. [30] and A.N.Emam et al. [31] and the findings of the TEM investigation and the crystalline size obtained are comparable.

To examine the fluorescence characteristics of the produced CQDs, UV light with a short wavelength (254 nm) is employed. When exposed to UV light, the generated CQDs displayed a green luminescence while having a light brown appearance during daylight exposure. The existence of CQD particles in the generated solution is suggested by the green luminescence under UV light. These findings recommended the authors to further examine the optical characteristics of CQDs.

In this investigation, the liquid phase-containing syringe-filtered CQDs are employed. The liquid phase-containing CQDs had an absorption capacity that exceeded the UV–vis spectrometer's maximum limit of 2.5. Consequently, ethanol, n-hexane, and DI H2O are used to dilute each of the original samples 5 times. For every run, a quartz cuvette containing 10 mL of diluted material is used to track absorbance between 200 and 600 nm in wavelength.

The absorption potential of the CQDs extracted from various solvents is examined with reference to the DI H2O blank run. The UV–vis spectra (absorption characteristics) illustrated in Fig. 3 show that UV–visible absorbance near IR spectra of (a) FC-extract and all the CQDS (b-d). The dimension and surface characteristics of CQDs determine the relative position of the absorption peak in UV–Vis spectra. The C = O bond's n - π* transition produces an absorption peak at 280–400 nm [32]. This is due to potential absorption sites and greater surface area, smaller CQDs can absorb more energy. The CQD samples varied in their peak intensities even though they all began to exhibit absorption characteristics at a similar wavelength (316 nm). For instance, the peak intensity for water is the highest at 2.85, while the intensities for ethanol and n-hexane are 2.15 and 1.98, respectively. Furthermore, the ethanol and n-hexane displayed an average distribution, but the water-derived CQDs displayed a normal distribution. According to this occurrence, W-CQDs can adsorb more specialized wavelengths among the three solvents examined, but CQDs derived from organic solvents can absorb a wider range of wavelengths. The specific transitions are commonly observed in UV–visible spectral regions which is mainly due to the electrons excitation from n→π* orbitals, respectively [33]. The bandgap of CQDs is established by employing the following formula (Eq. (1)).
(1)
E =
hc λ
where E is the band gap energy, h is Planck's constant (6.626 × 10−34 J s), λ is the wavelength, and c is the velocity of light (3 × 108 ms−1). Also, the band gap energies are calculated via Tauc's Plot (Fig. 4 ) as shown in the Table 1 . It is noted that the calculated and experimental band gap values exhibit close agreement. While lower band gaps enhance the absorption of a broader spectrum of light, an excessively low band gap can lead to higher charge carrier recombination due to the reduced energy offset between the CQDs and the perovskite layer. This can hinder efficient charge extraction and reduce overall device performance. Among the CQDs tested, those synthesized using water (W-CQDs) exhibited a moderate band gap (~3.47 eV), which offered an optimal trade-off-sufficient absorption in the UV–visible region while maintaining favorable energy level alignment with ZnO and CH3NH3PbI3 for effective electron transfer. This balance contributed to reduced recombination losses and enhanced charge transport across the ETL/perovskite interface, as reflected in the improved J-V parameters, particularly the short-circuit current density (Jsc) and power conversion efficiency (PCE). These observations clearly highlight the importance of tuning the band gap of CQDs through solvent engineering, demonstrating that precise band gap control plays a pivotal role in maximizing the performance of perovskite solar cells. The bandgap values obtained from Tauc's plots and UV–Vis absorption align well with previously reported values for biomass-derived CQDs. The moderate bandgap of W-CQDs offers an optimal balance between light absorption and charge transport, thereby enhancing photovoltaic efficiency [34,35].

The emission spectra of CQDs are assessed using PL analysis. Excitation-dependent photoluminescence is a unique characteristic of quantum dots that contradicts the Kasha-Vavilov rule. Each functional group within quantum dots has unique optimum excitation and emission bands. Blue, green, and yellow fluorescence have been associated with hydroxyl, ketone, and ester functional groups, respectively. Therefore, the bonding between the OH and COOH functional groups controls quantum dot fluorescence [36]. With great care, the excitation range is selected to lie within ±20 nm of the maximum absorbance, as indicated by the UV–Vis spectra shown in Fig. 5 . Water, ethanol, and n-hexane are used to assess the solvent-dependent behavior of CQDs in order to learn more about their molecular state. Similar to other reported CQD materials in the literature, the as-prepared CQDs and W-CQDs, E-QDs, and nH-CQDs in different aqueous solutions exhibited strong luminescence. As illustrated in the PL emission spectra, the CQDs in water have the largest fluorescence intensity, followed sequentially by n-hexane and ethanol. The peak luminosities of the W-CQDs, E-CQDs, and H-CQDs gradually changed to red, exhibiting a greater peak intensity. They showed a redshift, which indicated that the quantum dots included heteroatoms with various emissive sites. When the excitation wavelength exceeded 380, 400, and 460 nm for W-CQDs, E-CQDs, and nH-CQDs, This may be due to that excitation-dependent emission of CQDs is closely linked to their solvent-derived functional groups, size and surface states. The sharp decrease in PL beyond specific excitation thresholds suggests a reduced density of emissive states or quenching effects at higher excitation energies, highlighting the importance of selecting suitable excitation ranges for optimal luminescence. Additionally, adding CQDs to the ZnO ETL shows less charge recombination at the perovskite/ETL interface and effective charge transfer. CQDs minimize radiative recombination losses by enhancing electronic interaction and enabling the quick extraction of photo-generated electrons from the perovskite layer to the ETL. This is due to the strong interfacial electronic coupling and passivation effects introduced by surface-rich functional groups (e.g., –OH, –COOH) of CQDs, especially in W-CQDs. These functional groups form hydrogen bonds or coordinate with surface defects in ZnO and the perovskite layer, thereby minimizing trap states and facilitating efficient electron extraction. Furthermore, W-CQDs' numerous surface functional groups and smaller particle size facilitate improved passivation of surface defects and interfacial contact, facilitating smoother charge transport and improving charge carrier dynamics overall. The enhanced photovoltaic metrics, including better fill factor (FF) and short-circuit current density (Jsc), directly reflect these improvements, demonstrating the critical role that CQD inclusion plays in enabling efficient charge separation and conveyance. The emissive traps at the surfaces of bare CQDs often result in poor PL quantum yields and a comparatively higher absorption efficiency at long wavelengths.

To raise their PL quantum yields (QY) (Eq. (2)), they require a passivating layer. FTIR and XPS analyses indicate that the enhanced photoluminescence and redshift arise from surface states influenced by functional groups. The emission behavior was further validated by calculating the QY using a standard reference approach [37].

As shown in Fig. 5d the excitation wavelengths have differed from W-CQDs 280 to 480 nm, and the emission peaks have been red-shifted from ∼300 to ∼650 nm. The excitation-emission contour map in the 2D fluorescence spectra very clearly illustrates this phenomenon. Also, the excitation and emission map of W-CQDs shows multicolour photoluminescence properties. The photoexcitation of W-CQDs is due to π-plasmon absorption in the core carbon nanoparticles. For E-CQDs, the excitation wavelength ranged from 320 to 500 nm and emission peak red shifted from ∼350 to ∼650 nm (Fig. 5 e). Similarly, nH-CQDs have an excitation wavelength of 380–540 nm and emission peaks appeared around ∼400 to ∼650 nm (Fig. 5 f). Comparing the excitation-emission maps of the CQDs in various solvents, it can be observed that the PL emission peak of the nH-CQDs (at 528 nm) experienced a remarkable bathochromic shift of 68 nm as compared with W-CQDs (460 nm). The primary benefits of quantum dots over traditional organic or inorganic fluorophores include their exceptional photostability. Fig. 5g-i displays the PL intensity ratio (P/P0) of W-CQDs, E-CQDs, and nH-CQDs over time in conventional room settings and at the proper temperature (4 °C). To evaluate CQD photostability, the CQDs are exposed to both continuous UV light (365 nm, 16 W) and dark conditions. After 90 min in dark conditions, the CQDs showed no noticeable modifications, as shown in Fig. 5. Following the 90 min of UV light exposure, no reduction in fluorescence emission intensity was noticed, revealing the excellent photostability of CQDs, further indicating the great stability of the W-CQDs, E-CQDs, and nH-CQDs materials.

Raman analysis is used to confirm that the CQDs are sp
2 conjugated. Raman analysis has been widely employed to investigate the degree of graphitization and crystallinity of CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 6 the CQDs typically have two peaks at 1352 and 1585 cm−1 in their Raman spectra corresponding with the D band (sp
3 ) and G band (sp
2 ), respectively. These peaks correspond well with the values reported in the literature and are typical of carbon dots. The graphitic lattice defects or disorders in CQDs generated by surface oxidation inclusive of the sp
3 hybridized were responsible for the D band. The G band was caused by the hybridized sp
2 carbon network inside a hexagonal two-dimensional graphitic lattice and the first-order scattering of graphite E2g stretching mode of CQDs. The crystalline G band corresponding to the prominent peaks of W-CQDs, E-CQDs, and nH-CQDs in the Raman spectra at 1596, 1584, and 1582 cm−1 suggests the existence of disordered sp
2 carbon in the CC stretching mode. The disordered D band, neatly organized graphite of W-CQDs, E-CQDs, and nH-CQDs in the Raman spectra at 1335, 1328, and 1331 cm−1, respectively. The computed I
D
/I
G value is <2, which is consistent with numerous published results and indicates that the material is nanocrystalline graphitic. The structural features of carbon can be correlated using the intensity ratio (I
D
/I
G ) of the distinct Raman bands, with a relative intensity (I
D
/I
G ) of 0.84, the distinctive bands of CQDs indicate a greater concentration of graphitic structure but mostly GO and a lower proportion of crystalline nature. The maximum value of I
D
/I
G is 0.88 (Table 2 ) nH-CQDs could result from epoxy groups having flaws on the basal plane, which would explain the splitting into smaller particles and cause the weakness consistent with the FT-IR results.

From the Table 2, The I
D
/I
G ratios are less in W-CQDs because of the absence of CO and COOH groups. The same increases as these functional groups are more abundant at the edges of this sample in E-CQDs and nH-CQDs. The identical I

2
-sp
3 carbon, supporting effective electronic conductivity. At the ETL interface, this level of structural instability promotes improved charge transfer.

The surface functional groups of the fabricated CQDs could potentially be revealed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. Several investigations have revealed that CQDs contain a substantial concentration of oxygenated functional groups such as epoxy, ether, carboxyl, hydroxyl, etc., Each solvent shows the existence of various functional groups showing the variation in affinity of dissolving in various solvents. The W-CQDs show almost only two wider bands revealing the large presence of specific functional groups. In contrast, E-CQDs and nH-CQDs show only short and narrow bands confirming the small presence of many functional groups.

Fig. 7a-b illustrates the FTIR peaks of the obtained waste coffee powder extract and W-CQDs. The medium band at 1631 cm−1 and the small band at 2103 cm−1 are owing to the CN bond stretching, and a large band at 3305 cm−1 is related to the OH stretching.

Moreover, Fig. 7 c-d illustrates the E-CQDs and nH-CQDs samples' FTIR spectra, which display that few peaks are absorbed near the fingerprint region. The 437, 585, and 596 cm−1 small peaks indicated CO bond stretching [38]. The small band at 720 cm−1 is characteristic of –CH2/–CH3 bending vibrations groups. The medium peak at 844 cm−1 is assigned to the O-C=O bond and 964 cm−1 is associated with the epoxy groups' CC vibrations. The large narrower peak at 1042 cm−1 and the narrower band at 1088 cm−1 are owing to CO bond stretching. The tiny weak bands at 1376, 1381, 1421, 1443, 1652, and 1924 cm−1 are owing to the carboxylate stretch, CO, CO, CH, CC double bonds and CN asymmetric stretching [39]. The small peak at 2885 cm−1 is owing to symmetry and antisymmetric stretching of alkanes CH. The medium-narrow peak at 2970 cm−1 and the medium-wide peak at 3337 cm−1 may be owing to the vibrations of CH and OH hydroxyl stretching [40]. The medium peak at 1155 cm−1 is associated with the CO (alkoxy or epoxy) vibrations and the short peak at 1370 cm−1 is assigned to the vibration and deformation stretching of SO. The medium narrow peak at 1461 cm−1 is owing to the CH stretching, and a long narrow peak at 1737 cm−1 is related to the CO stretching. The medium peak at 2851 cm−1 and the long narrower peak at 2918 cm−1 correspond to the CH stretching. The tiny band at 3009 cm−1 is associated with the OH vibrations.

The HR-TEM morphologies and the diameter distribution of CQDs are illustrated in Fig. 7. It is noted that all CQDs are well-dispersed, possess nearly spherical shapes, and are arranged irregularly. The average particle diameter size ranges from 1.2 to 3.4 nm (based on a statistical analysis of >100 dots), as shown in Fig. 8 d-f established by the Gaussian fitting curve. The size distribution histogram of W-CQDs (Fig. 8 d) shows that the average diameter is approximately 1.95 ± 1.2 nm. Similarly, E-CQDs have an average diameter of 2.47 ± 1.6 nm, while nH-CQDs exhibit a particle size of around 2.34 ± 1.2 nm. The presence of graphitic carbon is established by its lattice width of 0.25 nm and the continuous identical fringe threads inside each dot in different orientations, demonstrating the diffraction planes of graphitic carbon. The HR-TEM image of a single W-CQDs particle illustrates a crystalline nature with a d-spacing value of 2.15 Å is associated with the (100) plane of graphite. Similarly, E-CQDs have a d-spacing value of 2.18 Å (100) and nH-CQDs show a d-spacing value of 2.2 Å (100). When the particle size is reduced below a critical size, a blue shift or an increase in the bandgap energy will be observed (it depends on the semiconductors). When the size of the nanoparticles decreases below a threshold level, the quantum-confinement effects become evident. Through the manipulation of CQD size, this effect enables tuning of the bandgap and other properties.

The elemental composition and chemical bonding of CQDs are examined by employing XPS analysis. The presence of C and O atoms in the cornstalks and as manufactured CQDs is confirmed by two distinct peaks in the survey scan (Fig. 9 a) at 285 and 531 eV, respectively, which are attributed to their 1 s orbitals. Two peaks at 288.4 eV and 284.7 eV can be seen in the deconvolution of the XPS spectra of the C1s level in CQDs (Fig. 9 b). These peaks are responsible for the CO, CO, and CC bonds. Acknowledging that graphitic substance exists is crucial because of the noticeable peak at around 284.7 eV. Furthermore, the O1s spectrum (Fig. 9 c) demonstrates two oxygen states of CO at 535.9 eV and C = O at 531.7 eV [41]. The atomic percentages of C and O are around 79.9 and 20.1 %, respectively, based on calculations. The XPS results verified that the W-CQDs' surfaces have hydrophilic functional groups, as reported in earlier research [42]. The dominance of CO and CO groups, which are essential for interface bonding and defect passivation, is confirmed by XPS spectra. These results support the enhanced performance of CQD-modified devices and are in line with FTIR data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical properties of CH3NH3PbI3 material
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 displays the XRD pattern of perovskite thin films. The three major peaks (2θ = 14.450, 28.660, and 40.910) correspond to the (110), (220) and (224) planes. These results strongly confirm the presence of the tetragonal phase CH3NH3PbI3 material with space group 14/mcm [43]. In addition, the micro-Raman spectrum of CH3NH3PbI3 material is shown in Fig. 10 b.

The MA cation is represented by the peak seen at 138 cm−1. Torsional vibrations are responsible for additional peaks at 282 cm−1 and 328 cm−1, which show the existence of material domains with different levels of organization [44]. Fig. 10 c shows the UV–Vis spectra, which revealed the CH3NH3PbI3 material's broad absorption properties in the 200–800 nm range. The absorption edges of the perovskite material move in the direction of the longer wavelength. Moreover, Tauc's plot eq. (1) can be used to determine the bang gap of perovskite absorber material. The CH3NH3PbI3 crystalline films' obtained band gap values (Eg = 1.23 eV) are in good agreement with previously reported values [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Perovskite solar cell performance and stability analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The fabricated planar structure perovskite solar cell configuration of FTO/ZnO/CQDs/CH3NH3PbI3/carbon device analyzed under simulated solar irradiation of AM 1.5G (100 MW cm−2) as shown in Fig. 11 . It shows that the different solvent extracted CQDs incorporated perovskite photovoltaic device performance evaluated the below Eqs. (3) and (4). The fill factor (FF) and power conversion efficiency (η) are determined using the formulae.

Eman F. Sawires et al. reported that the incorporation of nitrogen-doped quantum dots (N-QDs) into various layers of perovskite solar cells significantly improved charge transport, suppressed charge recombination, and enhanced device stability, resulting in a notable increase in power conversion efficiency (PCE) up to 22.5 % [2].Tingting Xuet al. reported that the PSC efficiency of 13.28 % and 10.50 % for using A-CQD and CA-CQD, respectively [46]. Hebing Tang reported that the carbon-based perovskite solar cell (C-PSC), when passivated with an A-CQDs film, demonstrated enhanced photovoltaic performance, achieving a peak power conversion efficiency of 13.97 %. In comparison, the PCEs for the control PSCs and those incorporating a CA-CQDs layer were recorded at 11.29 % and 10.77 %, respectively [47]. The incorporation of high-quality carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) into the perovskite and mesoporous layers of PSCs resulted in a significant enhancement in power conversion efficiency (from 16.57 % to 20.44 %), increased photocurrent, an improved fill factor (from 70 % to 75 %), and superior device stability compared to pristine PSCs, as reported by Masfer Alkahtani et al. [48]. Table ST: Comparation of carbon dots modified perovskite solar cell performance [27,46,49–51].

Eman F. Sawires et al. reported that the incorporation of nitrogen-doped quantum dots (N-QDs) into various layers of perovskite solar cells significantly improved charge transport, suppressed charge recombination, and enhanced device stability, resulting in a notable increase in power conversion efficiency (PCE) up to 22.5 % [2].Tingting Xuet al. reported that the PSC efficiency of 13.28 % and 10.50 % for using A-CQD and CA-CQD, respectively [46]. Hebing Tang reported that the carbon-based perovskite solar cell (C-PSC), when passivated with an A-CQDs film, demonstrated enhanced photovoltaic performance, achieving a peak power conversion efficiency of 13.97 %. In comparison, the PCEs for the control PSCs and those incorporating a CA-CQDs layer were recorded at 11.29 % and 10.77 %, respectively [47]. The incorporation of high-quality carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) into the perovskite and mesoporous layers of PSCs resulted in a significant enhancement in power conversion efficiency (from 16.57 % to 20.44 %), increased photocurrent, an improved fill factor (from 70 % to 75 %), and superior device stability compared to pristine PSCs, as reported by Masfer Alkahtani et al. [48]. Table ST: Comparation of carbon dots modified perovskite solar cell performance [27,46,49–51].

Normally, the incorporation of CQDs into the ETL layer of PSCs also supports to decrease of hysteresis. It is often attributed to charge trapping and ion migration at the interfacial layers. Also, passivation, charge trapping, and recombination barriers are minimized hysteresis [2]. The combination of these effects contributes to the higher efficiency observed in CQD-enhanced devices compared to those with a ZnO layer alone. Apart from this, different solvent-extracted carbon quantum dots also play a major role in improving the efficiencies. When water, ethanol, and n-hexane are extracted carbon quantum dots have variable particle sizes (1.95 ± 1.2 nm; 2.47 ± 1.6 nm, and 2.34 ± 1.2 nm) which reflects the device performances. According to the principles of quantum confinement, CQDs with smaller particle sizes exhibit altered electronic structures, leading to discrete energy levels and more efficient charge carrier dynamics. In this study, the W-CQDs displayed the smallest average particle size (~1.95 nm), which correlates with improved charge extraction efficiency. This enhancement arises because smaller CQDs have a larger surface-to-volume ratio, facilitating more effective interaction with the ZnO and perovskite layers at the ETL interface. Additionally, PL analysis revealed that W-CQDs exhibited lower emission intensity compared to E-CQDs and nH-CQDs. This reduced PL intensity indicates suppressed radiative recombination, suggesting that more photogenerated carriers are being transferred rather than lost to recombination.

When the size of CQDs falls below their exciton Bohr radius, their optical and electronic properties are significantly influenced by size-dependent quantum effects. This happens due to changes in the energy levels within the core made up of sp
2 ‑carbon structures [52]. The combination of quantum confinement and strong interfacial bonding via surface functional groups thus plays a dual role-enhancing both charge transport and photovoltaic stability. These observations strongly support the use of small-sized, surface-functionalized CQDs especially W-CQDs as interfacial modifiers to boost both the efficiency and long-term stability of PSCs. This strategy represents a scalable and eco-friendly approach for advancing next-generation photovoltaic technologies.

Fig. 11b illustrates the ambient stability analysis of PSCs, where the devices were stored under ambient conditions for 500 h without encapsulation. The ZnO-based PSCs exhibited an initial degradation in PCE from 9.09 % to 7.6 % after 500 h. Similarly, PSCs incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) also demonstrated efficiency degradation; however, the extent of degradation was lower compared to devices without CQDs. Among the CQD-based devices, nH-CQDs retained a PCE of 9.45 % after 500 h, while E-CQDs and W-CQDs maintained higher efficiencies of 10.44 % and 11.48 %, respectively. The degradation percentages for ZnO, E-CQDs, nH-CQDs, and W-CQDs were 16.39 %, 8.66 %, 5.87 %, and 1.96 %, respectively, indicating that W-CQDs exhibited the least degradation. This enhanced stability can be attributed to the enhanced interface contact between the perovskite layer and the ETL, which facilitates more robust charge transfer and minimizes interface-related defects. Moreover, the superior film quality of the perovskite layer with CQDs acts as an effective barrier against airborne water molecules, preventing their infiltration and thereby improving overall stability [53].

Generally, the conducting substrate-deposited ZnO/CH3NH3PbI3 interface initiates a degradation mechanism due to the interaction between the CH₃NH₃PbI₃ and the ZnO layer. This interaction leads to the weakening of the bond between the lead iodide (PbI2) anion and the methyl ammonium (CH3NH3 +) cation, facilitated by hydroxyl and water molecules. Under AM 1.5 light illumination, hydroxyl radicals generated on the ZnO surface rapidly damage the CH₃NH₃PbI₃ lattice structure, resulting in the separation of CH3NH3 + and PbI2, as reported in previous studies [14,54–56]. Similarly, under ambient atmospheric conditions, water molecules (H2O) are adsorbed onto the ZnO surface due to its hydrophilic nature. This adsorption can trap water molecules at the ZnO surface, leading to the dehydration of the perovskite layer. Initially, water absorption forms a hydrated phase, but upon further exposure, the dark brown perovskite is regenerated. However, once the ZnO surface becomes saturated with moisture, the perovskite is expected to rapidly decompose. Moreover, CH3NH3 + cations can convert into hydronium ions (H₃O+), while methyl ammonium may convert into CH3NH3I [54,57,58]. To overcome these issues and reduce the ZnO/perovskite interfacial contact, carbon quantum dots are introduced. The incorporation of carbon QDs reduces perovskite degradation and enhances charge transfer properties under light illumination. The carbon QDs act as a charge-transfer bridge at the ZnO/perovskite interface, thereby improving device stability under ambient conditions (as shown in Fig. 11(b) and Table 3 ).

In this study, CQDs extracted from waste coffee grounds were used in different solvents, such as n-hexane, ethanol, and water. The proposed mechanism of the interaction between the ETL/CQDs/perovskite structure is shown in Fig. 12 . The experimental results indicate CQDs play a significant role in improving the efficiency of the fabricated PSCs. The CH and OH functional groups present in the aforementioned solvents are responsible for charge transfer at the interfaces of the ZnO/CQDs/perovskite layers (i.e., CH3NH3PbI3). According to previous literature, it is noted that OH groups exhibit strong interactions with the perovskite and ZnO layers, likely due to the electronegative oxygen atom, which enables dipole interactions with the ionic or polar components of the perovskite. Moreover, the OH groups can chemically bond with the surface of the ZnO/perovskite interface, improving adhesion and interfacial contact. In contrast, the C-H groups, being less polar, do not form a robust interface with the perovskite layer [46]. Under solar light irradiation, photo-induced charge transfer occurs in the CQDs decorated on the ZnO surface. During this process, the excited electron is transferred to charge transfer states shared between the components, primarily localized within the CQDs at the conduction band of ZnO, while the hole remains electronically and structurally close to the ZnO [59]. This separation ultimately reduces the recombination of electron-hole pairs, ultimately enhancing the efficiency of the perovskite solar cells. Additionally, the CQDs decorated on ZnO reduce cracks, voids, and pinholes in the ZnO layer surface, preventing short circuits caused by the infiltration of the perovskite material. In the proposed mechanism is supported by literature on CQDs and ZnO band alignment [46,59], highlighting reduced recombination and enhanced interfacial charge extraction. Future studies will include impedance analysis to further validate this model.

In summary, we developed a hybrid electron transport layer (ZnO-ETL) by incorporating carbon dots extracted from coffee grounds using water, ethanol, and n-hexane solvents. The CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite absorber solution was prepared by the solution process. These materials reveal the optical, structural, and morphological properties demonstrated by several characterizations, CQDs incorporated the ZnO layer, enhanced charge transport, and reduced surface defects at the ETL/perovskite interface in planar PPv. This modified ETL structure facilitates the formation of homogeneous crystalline and uniform perovskite crystals in the absorber films, contributing to improved device performance. The integration of CQDs into the ZnO ETL effectively addresses interfacial defects and enhances charge transfer properties. Additionally, the small size (1.8 nm) W-CQDs serve as a charge transfer bridge between the ETL and CH3NH3PbI3 interfaces. Consequently, the incorporation of W-CQDs significantly boosts the PCE of planar PPv, resulting in an impressive increase from 9.09 % to 11.71 %. Notably enhances the Jsc and Voc of planar PSCs, marking a significant step forward in their performance. Additionally, the PSC devices exhibit remarkable stability, maintaining efficient performance for 500 h with minimal degradation when exposed to 1.5 solar irradiation. The research highlights the use of sustainable, non-toxic CQDs derived from eco-friendly sources, which prove to be highly effective in optimizing CH3NH3PbI3-based planar PPv applications. This development underscores the potential for these materials to advance the field of renewable energy technologies.
